beats.title: THE CAMPAIGN BRIEFING
beats.subtitle: One real system, end to end · Aegis Regional Hospital
say: VEGA
Before we study the campaign step by step, let's watch a real one unfold — start to finish.
NOVA
A real system? Not the metaphor?
VEGA
A real system. And this time, apprentice, you're running it.

beats.title: Meet the team
bullets: Maya — System Owner: accountable for the system day to day
The CIO — Authorizing Official (AO): signs to accept the risk
Raj — ISSO: the hands-on security officer for this system
Sam — Privacy Officer: guards the patients' personal data
Stellaris Audit Co. — the independent Assessor
say: VEGA

Security isn't done by one hero. It's a team: Maya owns the system day to day; the CIO is the authorizing official who signs for the risk; Raj is the hands-on security officer; Sam guards the patients' privacy; and an outside firm, Stellaris, assesses the work.
NOVA
So 'doing 800-53' is really a group of people, each with a named role and hat.

beats.plain: Aegis Regional Hospital runs a Patient Records System: a web app plus a database, used by about 800 clinicians, hosted on-premises, holding patients' medical records.
beats.example: Web app + database + 800 users + sensitive health records = one information system to authorize.
say: VEGA
Here's our system. Not just a computer — an app, a database, the staff who use it, and the data they protect.
NOVA
So those records count as PII — that means extra rules, right?

beats.title: Step 1–2: Prepare, then Categorize the impact
say: VEGA
First, prepare — set roles and context. Then categorize: how bad is the worst case for C, I, and A?
VEGA
Confidentiality, Moderate. But Integrity is High — a wrong dose could kill. Availability is High — downtime closes the ER.
NOVA
And the high-water mark takes the worst of the three...
VEGA
So the whole system is categorized High. One High pulls it all up.

beats.title: Step 3: Select the starting controls
bullets: The HIGH security baseline from SP 800-53B — 370 controls and enhancements
PLUS the Privacy baseline (96) — because the system processes PII
This is the starting kit, not the finished list
say: VEGA
Maya opens 800-53B and draws the High baseline — and the privacy baseline, because of those patient records.
NOVA
A starting kit, sized to the stakes.

beats.plain: A control someone else provides for you, so you don't build it yourself. You 'inherit' it. Controls can be common (provided), system-specific (yours alone), or hybrid (shared).
beats.example: PE-3 (physical access) is common (the data center). AC-6 (least privilege) is system-specific. CP-2 (contingency plan) is often hybrid.
say: NOVA
Does Maya's team really configure every one of 370 controls?
VEGA
No. The data center already provides physical security — control PE-3 — for every tenant. Maya inherits it — that's a common control.

beats.title: Step 4: Tailor the baseline to fit
bullets: Scope out only what truly doesn't apply: this app is wired-only, so AC-18 (Wireless Access) is out of its boundary
Inherit PE-3 (physical access) from the data center — a common control
Add controls from a healthcare (HIPAA) overlay
Set parameters: AC-2 disables inactive accounts after 60 days
say: VEGA
Tailoring is fitting the kit to the system — not an excuse to delete protections.
NULL
I look for the one control you wrongly tailored away.
VEGA
Which is why every change is justified and written down — we drop wireless only because this system has none.

beats.title: Step 5: Implement the controls
bullets: IA-2: multi-factor login for all 800 clinicians
SC-28: encrypt the patient database at rest
SI-2: patch servers on a set schedule
CP-9: a nightly backup kept offline, where ransomware can't reach it
say: VEGA
Now Raj's team puts the controls to work. The catalog says what to do; they choose how to do it.
NOVA
So the catalog says what to do — but Raj's team picks how?

beats.title: Step 6: Assess — do they actually work?
bullets: Stellaris (independent) EXAMINES the security plan
INTERVIEWS Raj and the admins
TESTS the system: a vulnerability scan and a phishing simulation
The findings become a Security Assessment Report — the SAR
say: VEGA
An outside assessor checks the work three ways: examine, interview, test.
NOVA
Wait — so just picking a control isn't proof? Someone has to check it works?

beats.plain: The risk that's left after your controls are in place. It never reaches zero — so a senior leader has to look at what remains and decide if it's acceptable.
say: NOVA
What about the weaknesses the assessor found?
VEGA
The risk that remains — residual risk. The CIO weighs it before anyone goes live.

beats.title: Step 7–8: Authorize, then Monitor forever
bullets: The CIO reviews the SAR, accepts the residual risk, signs a 3-year ATO
12 open findings go on a POA&M — each with an owner and a due date
Monitor: quarterly scans, annual reviews, re-authorize after the big upgrade
A system is never simply 'done'
say: VEGA
The CIO signs the authorization to operate. Then the watch begins — and never ends.
NULL
Cute — a checklist against me? I live in the gaps between your steps.

beats.title: The campaign, in one breath
lines: Categorize the system (HIGH) → select a baseline (800-53B) → tailor it to fit.
Implement the controls → assess them (examine/interview/test → SAR).
Authorize (the ATO, accepting residual risk; gaps → POA&M) → monitor forever.
beats.mnemonic: One system, one team, seven steps — that's the RMF.
say: NOVA
I just watched a whole system get secured, start to finish.
VEGA
You did. Now we'll slow down and learn each step in depth — starting with the framework itself.
